Unit Project 02

Helennah Tran

February 6, 2026

title: “Lecture 06 – ggplot2”

author: “Helennah Tran”

date: “2026-01-27”

output: html_document

Q1

Which island has the most penguins?

Answer: Biscoe has the most penguins.

What changes visually?

Answer: The background with theme_minimal made it white on the plot.


``` r
ggplot(penguins, aes(x = island)) +
  geom_bar() +
  labs(
    title = "Penguin observations by island",
    x = "Island",
    y = "Count"
  ) + theme_minimal()

# Q2: # What is the general shape of the distribution (normal? bimodal?) # Answer: In this histogram, the general shape is bimodal because it doesn’t have one clear peak. # How does bin number change what you see? # Answer: A lower bins shows a more smoother and blocky and individual peaks seem to merge better. Higher bins show higher fine details and looks more jagged. # How does the distribution differ between species? Do they all have the same approximate mean? # Answer: Adelie have the lowest body mass, chinstrap have intermediate body mass, and gentoo penguins have the highest body mass. No, the species do not have the same approximate mean.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Penguin Body Mass", x = "Body Mass (g)", y = "Count") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass Distribution by Species", x = "Body Mass (g)", y = "Count" ) + theme_minimal()

#Q3 #Which species has the longest flippers (typical value)? # Answer: Gentoo penguins have the highest flipper length.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Flipper Length by Species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Flipper Length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Do males and females differ? # Answer: Yes, males have a higher body mass.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass by Sex",
    x = "Sex",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Does it help readability? # Answer: Yes, it adds colors.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(
    title = "Flipper Length by Species (Colored)",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Flipper Length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Q4 # Is the relationship positive, negative, or unclear? # Answer: The relationship is unclear.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Depth vs Bill Length",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Does that change your interpretation? # Answer: Yes, because there’s color.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Depth vs Bill Length by Species",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Does the line fit the data well? # Answer: Yes, the line does fit the data well.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill Depth vs Bill Length with Linear Fit",
    x = "Bill Length (mm)",
    y = "Bill Depth (mm)"
 ) +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

# Q5 # Which species shows the strongest relationship? # Answer: Gentoo shows the strongest relationship.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass vs Flipper Length by Species",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# What patterns appear? # Answer: Males have a higher body mass and gentoos are heavier.

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g)) +
  geom_point() +
  facet_grid(sex ~ species) +
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass vs Flipper Length by Sex and Species",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

#Q6

ggplot(penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = sex)) +geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +facet_wrap(~ species) + labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Flipper Length and Body Mass in Penguins", 
 x = "Flipper Length (mm)",y = "Body Mass (g)",color = "Sex") + theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

#Q7 # What was the most fun or interesting part of this exercise? Which part was most confusing or difficult? # Answer: Looking at the different colors and plots were fun! The syntax of ggplots was confusing.

title: “Lecture 07: Regular Expressions”

author: “Helennah Tran”

date: “2026-01-29”

output: html_document

Q1: Run each of the below commands and write (in words) what each one does.

Answer: The first command checks the word adelie appears in the species column and returns TRUE or FALSE for each row.

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie")
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE

Answer: The second command removes all question marks from the sex column.

str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?", "")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [37] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [55] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [73] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f"
##  [91] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [109] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [127] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [145] "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [163] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M"
## [181] "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f"
## [199] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [217] "f" "M" NA  "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [235] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
## [253] "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  "M"
## [271] "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [289] "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [307] "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [325] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M"
## [343] "M" "f"

Answer: The third command removes extra spaces and collapses repeated whitespace in the island column.

str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)
##   [1] "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen"
##   [7] "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen" "Torgersen"

Q2

Answer: The number is 316.

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))

penguins_messy %>%
  pull(valid_id) %>%
  sum()
## [1] 316

Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id)
head(penguins_messy)
##   species_messy island_messy sex_messy sample_id
## 1        adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-535
## 2        adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-442
## 3        adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-668
## 4        adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-920
## 5        adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-223
## 6        adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-899
##                                   file_name
## 1 2024-01-14_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-535.csv
## 2 2024-01-17_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-442.csv
## 3 2024-01-08_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-668.csv
## 4 2024-01-26_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-920.csv
## 5 2024-01-06_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-223.csv
## 6 2024-01-08_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-899.csv
##                                                    notes valid_id
## 1 id=PAL-535; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.1mm     TRUE
## 2 id=PAL-442; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.5mm     TRUE
## 3 id=PAL-668; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=40.3mm     TRUE
## 4   id=PAL-920; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=NAmm     TRUE
## 5 id=PAL-223; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=36.7mm     TRUE
## 6 id=PAL-899; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.3mm     TRUE

Q4

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(
    file_date = str_extract(file_name, "[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"),
    file_id   = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}")
  )

head(penguins_messy, 10)
##    species_messy island_messy sex_messy sample_id
## 1         adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-535
## 2         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-442
## 3         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-668
## 4         adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-920
## 5         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-223

## 7         adelie  Torgersen\t        f?   PAL-268
## 8         adelie  Torgersen\t         M   PAL-139
## 9         adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-847
## 10        adelie  Torgersen\t      <NA>   PAL-238
##                                    file_name
## 1  2024-01-14_Torgersen\t_adelie_PAL-535.csv

## 3  id=PAL-668; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=40.3mm     TRUE 2024-01-08

## 6  id=PAL-899; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.3mm     TRUE 2024-01-08
## 7  id=PAL-268; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=38.9mm     TRUE 2024-01-08
## 8  id=PAL-139; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=39.2mm     TRUE 2024-01-19
## 9  id=PAL-847; island=Torgersen\t; sp=adelie; bill=34.1mm     TRUE 2024-01-14

## 9  PAL-847

Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))

unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(sex = sex_messy %>%str_replace_all("\\?", "") %>%str_to_upper() )
unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(bill_mm = notes %>%str_replace_all(".*bill=", "") %>%str_replace_all("mm", "") %>%as.numeric())
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
## ℹ In argument: `bill_mm = `%>%`(...)`.
## Caused by warning in `notes %>% str_replace_all(".*bill=", "") %>% str_replace_all("mm", "") %>%
##     as.numeric()`:
## ! NAs introduced by coercion
head(penguins_messy$bill_mm, 10)
##  [1] 39.1 39.5 40.3   NA 36.7 39.3 38.9 39.2 34.1 42.0

#Q8 # What was the most fun or interesting part of this exercise? Which part was most confusing or difficult? # Answer: The most interesting part was cleaning up messy data with regular expressions. The most confusing part was how to write special characters and regular expressions. # Do you feel like you have a general idea of how regular expressions are used? # I have a general idea but I need more practice.

title: “Lecture 08 – The Data Wrangling Survival Kit”

author: “Helennah Tran”

date: “2026-02-03”

output: html_document

#Q1 # Answer: There are 344 rows of original penguins and 333 penguins_clean.

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)

nrow(penguins)
## [1] 333
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

#Q2 Why can duplicates mess up summary statitics and plots? # Answer: Duplicates can affect summary results because repeated rows are counted more than once.

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE)
  )
nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
penguins_nodup <- penguins_dup %>%distinct()

nrow(penguins_nodup)
## [1] 333

#Q3 How many columns are in the resulting data frame? # Answer: There are 8 columns.

penguins_selected <- penguins_clean %>%
  select(species,island,sex,where(is.numeric))
ncol(penguins_selected)
## [1] 8

#Q4

penguins_clean %>%
  select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(matches("bill|flipper"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

#Q5 Why is across(where(is.numeric), …) better than typing out every numeric column name by hand? # Answer: Using across(where(is.numeric), …) is better because it automatically applies the calculation to all numeric columns.

penguins_clean %>%group_by(species) %>%
  summarise(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

#Q6

penguins_clean <- penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(bill_length = case_when(
      bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
      bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
      bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long"
    )
  )

#Q7 What is the number of columns in penguins_instrumented and penguins_clean? # penguins_clean has 9 columns. # penguins_instrumented has 12 columns.

instrument_meta <- tibble(instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"), calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0), manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%mutate(instrument_id = case_when(year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C")) %>%left_join(instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")
ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 9
ncol(penguins_instrumented)
## [1] 12

#Q8 # What was the most fun or interesting part of this exercise? Which part was most confusing or difficult? # Answer: The most interesting part was creating new variables. The most confusing part was keeping track of how each step changed the number of rows or columns after filtering and joining. # Do you feel like these general “survival tips” will be useful in your data analyses in the future? # Answer: Yes, it will be useful in data analyses in the future because it provides a clear way to show data, variables, results, and combine everything.
